sentences.base_language: Luis is a programmer and works a lot during the week.
sentences.target_language: Luis es programador y trabaja mucho durante la semana.
sentences.grammarNote: **Es** (ser) for professions: soy médico, es programador — no article before a profession after ser. Compare: **está** (estar) for temporary states. **Trabaja** = he works (3rd person present of -ar verb trabajar). **Durante** = during — always followed by a noun.

sentences.base_language: On Saturday morning, he woke up at seven.
sentences.target_language: El sábado por la mañana, se despertó a las siete.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se despertó** = he woke up — preterite of the reflexive verb **despertarse**. The reflexive pronoun **se** always goes before the conjugated verb. Preterite -ó ending marks 3rd person singular of -ar verbs.
**Por la mañana** = in the morning (part of the day). **A las siete** = at seven — always **a las** for time, except **a la una** (at one).

sentences.base_language: He was very hungry and wanted to go to the grocery store.
sentences.target_language: Tenía mucha hambre y quería ir al supermercado.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tenía** and **quería** are in the **imperfect tense** — used for ongoing states, feelings, and desires in the past. Compare with the preterite (a completed action): **fue** (he went) vs **quería ir** (he wanted to go — background desire).
**Tener hambre** = to be hungry, lit. 'to have hunger' — the same for: tener sed (thirsty), tener frío (cold), tener miedo (afraid). **Al** = a + el (contraction, required before masculine nouns).

sentences.base_language: He washed his face, got dressed, and left home.
sentences.target_language: Se lavó la cara, se vistió y salió de casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se lavó la cara** = washed his face — reflexive + definite article for body parts (not the possessive *su cara*). This is standard in Spanish: me lavo las manos (I wash my hands), se cepilló los dientes (he brushed his teeth).
**Se vistió** = got dressed — preterite of **vestirse** (stem-changing e→i in preterite: vistió, vistieron). **Salió** = left (preterite of salir, irregular).

sentences.base_language: The grocery store was nearby — only a ten-minute walk.
sentences.target_language: El supermercado estaba cerca, a solo diez minutos a pie.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estaba** = was (imperfect of estar) — for location and descriptions of ongoing states in the past: el café estaba cerrado (the café was closed).
**A pie** = on foot — a fixed expression. **A solo diez minutos** = only ten minutes away: the preposition **a** expresses distance or time: a dos kilómetros (two kilometers away), a una hora (an hour away).

sentences.base_language: There he bought eggs, vegetables, and chicken.
sentences.target_language: Allí compró huevos, verduras y pollo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Compró** = he bought — preterite of **comprar** (regular -ar verb): compré, compraste, compró, compramos, compraron.
**Allí** (there) at the start of a sentence anchors the new location after the previous sentence established travel. In Spanish lists: A, B y C (no comma before y, unlike English). Note: no article before huevos, verduras, pollo — when talking about items in general, uncount usage drops the article.

sentences.base_language: He also grabbed some fruit: apples and bananas.
sentences.target_language: También tomó algunas frutas: manzanas y plátanos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**También** = also/too — placed before the verb or at the start of a clause. **Tomó** = took/grabbed (preterite of tomar). **Tomar** is very versatile in Spanish: tomar el autobús (take the bus), tomar el desayuno (have breakfast), tomar una foto (take a photo).
**Algunas** = some — feminine plural agreeing with **frutas**. The four forms: algún, alguna, algunos, algunas.

sentences.base_language: After finishing, he sent his friend a message.
sentences.target_language: Al terminar, le mandó un mensaje a su amigo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Al + infinitive** = upon/after doing something — one of the most useful Spanish constructions: al llegar (upon arriving), al salir (when leaving), al terminar (after finishing). It expresses two events in close sequence.
**Le mandó** = sent to him — **le** is the indirect object pronoun (to him/her). The structure: **le** + verb + direct object + **a** + person: le mandó un mensaje a su amigo (he sent his friend a message).

sentences.base_language: His friend replied: "I'm waiting for you at the café!"
sentences.target_language: Su amigo respondió: "¡Te espero en el café!"
sentences.grammarNote:
**Te espero** = I'm waiting for you — **esperar** takes a direct object here (you). The present tense in Spanish often expresses an ongoing action or an immediate commitment: te espero = I'm waiting for you (right now).
**¡...!** — Spanish uses an inverted exclamation mark **¡** at the start of an exclamation. **En el café** = at the café — **en** for location inside/at a place.

sentences.base_language: Luis left the bags at home and went to the café.
sentences.target_language: Luis dejó las bolsas en casa y fue al café.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dejó** = left/put down (preterite of dejar). **Fue** = went — the preterite of **ir** is completely irregular: fui, fuiste, fue, fuimos, fueron. Crucially, it is identical to the preterite of **ser** (to be) — context tells them apart.
**En casa** = at home (no article). **Al café** = to the café (a + el contraction).

sentences.base_language: His friend Sofía was already sitting there, with a smile on her face.
sentences.target_language: Su amiga Sofía ya estaba sentada allí, con una sonrisa en la cara.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ya estaba sentada** = was already sitting — **ya** (already) + imperfect of estar + past participle as adjective. **Estaba sentada** describes a state (being in a seated position), not the action of sitting down (which would be se sentó).
**Con una sonrisa en la cara** = with a smile on her face — **en la cara** uses the definite article, not the possessive. Body parts and facial features take the article in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: Sofía asked him: "How are you? You look tired."
sentences.target_language: Sofía le preguntó: "¿Cómo estás? Te ves cansado."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le preguntó** = asked him — **le** as indirect object (the person being asked). **¿Cómo estás?** uses **estar** because it asks about current state, not permanent identity (which would use ser).
**Te ves cansado** = you look tired — **verse** (reflexive of ver) = to look/appear: te ves bien (you look well), se ve contento (he seems happy). This is different from **estar cansado** (to be tired) — verse + adjective describes appearance.

sentences.base_language: Luis sighed and said: "Work has me stressed — there's a lot of pressure."
sentences.target_language: Luis suspiró y dijo: "El trabajo me tiene estresado, hay mucha presión."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me tiene estresado** = has me stressed — a vivid construction: [noun] + tener + [object pronoun] + [adjective]: el ruido me tiene loco (the noise is driving me crazy), el trabajo me tiene agotado (work has me exhausted).
**Dijo** = said (preterite of decir, irregular: dije, dijiste, dijo...). **Hay** = there is/are — invariable; doesn't change for singular or plural: hay un problema, hay muchos problemas.

sentences.base_language: Sofía said: "You need to relax! Let's go to the park for a little walk."
sentences.target_language: Sofía dijo: "¡Necesitas relajarte! Vamos al parque a caminar un poco."
sentences.grammarNote:
**Necesitas relajarte** = you need to relax — when a reflexive verb follows another verb, the pronoun attaches to the infinitive: necesitas relajarte, quiero divertirme, debes cuidarte.
**Vamos a** + infinitive = let's go + verb / we're going to + verb (context determines which): ¡Vamos a comer! (Let's eat!). **Un poco** after a verb = a little: caminar un poco (walk a little), descansar un poco (rest a bit).

sentences.base_language: After finishing their coffees, they went to the park together.
sentences.target_language: Después de tomarse el café, fueron juntos al parque.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de + infinitive** = after doing something: después de comer (after eating), después de llegar (after arriving). Compare: **al + infinitive** (upon doing, simultaneous) vs **después de** (after, sequential).
**Tomarse el café** — the reflexive se added to tomar gives a sense of enjoying or completing the action: tomarse un descanso (take a break), tomarse su tiempo (take one's time). **Fueron** = they went (preterite of ir, irregular).

sentences.base_language: The park air was fresh — Luis took a deep breath and felt much better.
sentences.target_language: El aire del parque era fresco; Luis respiró profundo y se sintió mucho mejor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Era** = was (imperfect of ser) — for describing qualities and characteristics in the past. **Respiró profundo** = breathed deeply — **profundo** used as an adverb without -mente, common in everyday speech: hablar claro (speak clearly), correr rápido (run fast).
**Se sintió** = felt (preterite of sentirse, stem-changing e→i). **Mucho mejor** = much better — **mucho** intensifies comparatives: mucho más grande (much bigger), mucho peor (much worse).

sentences.base_language: They walked through the park for an hour and talked about everything.
sentences.target_language: Caminaron por el parque durante una hora y hablaron de todo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Caminaron / hablaron** = they walked / talked — preterite, 3rd person plural (-aron). **Por** = through/for: caminar por el parque (walk through the park), por una hora (for an hour — though **durante una hora** is more precise for duration).
**Hablar de** = to talk about: hablar de todo (talk about everything), hablar de trabajo (talk about work). **De todo** = about everything / of everything.

sentences.base_language: Walking home, Luis's body felt a bit sore, but his spirits were very good.
sentences.target_language: Al volver a casa, Luis sentía el cuerpo un poco adolorido, pero el ánimo muy bueno.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Al volver** = upon/when returning (al + infinitive). **Sentía** = felt (imperfect — describing an ongoing background feeling, not a single event). **Adolorido** = achy/sore — from **dolor** (pain).
**El ánimo** = spirits/mood/morale: estar de buen ánimo (to be in good spirits), levantar el ánimo (to lift someone's spirits). Compare: **estado de ánimo** (mood/emotional state) — a fuller form of the same idea.

sentences.base_language: He decided to go out with friends every weekend from now on.
sentences.target_language: Decidió salir con amigos todos los fines de semana de ahora en adelante.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Decidió** = decided (preterite of decidir, regular -ir verb). **Salir con** = to go out with (a person). **Todos los fines de semana** = every weekend — the plural of **el fin de semana**.
**De ahora en adelante** = from now on — a very useful fixed expression. Compare: **a partir de ahora** (starting now), **en el futuro** (in the future). The preterite **decidió** captures the moment of decision; what follows describes the plan.

sentences.base_language: That night, he fell asleep very quickly and slept wonderfully.
sentences.target_language: Esa noche, se durmió muy rápido y durmió de maravilla.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se durmió** = fell asleep — reflexive preterite of **dormirse** (to fall asleep). Compare: **durmió** (he slept, no reflexive) for the act of sleeping. The reflexive marks the transition into sleep.
**De maravilla** = wonderfully, like a dream — a vivid fixed expression: funciona de maravilla (it works wonderfully), me fue de maravilla (it went great for me). Compare: **bien** (well), **muy bien** (very well), **de maravilla** (amazingly).
